items.question: Out of all of the university programs you applied to, how would you rank Waterloo SE?
Which extracurriculars were you part of in high school?
How many years of programming experience did you have prior to starting 1A?
Did you participate in any high school enrichment programs?
How many people did you know from high school that also went to Waterloo?
Did you take a gap year between high school and university?
If you took a gap year, why did you choose to take one?

answers.label: There were a lot of factors: High school burnout, wanted to take a break before I went into another 4 years of education. Desire to travel (that didn't work out lmao). Wanted to meet relatives one last time. Wanted to strengthen my programming skills more outside of school before heading straight in. Wanted to "find myself" a bit more through things I didn't have the chance to do in high school. Didn't want to leave the city because my best friends at the time were staying.

labels: There were a lot of factors: High school burnout, wanted to take a break before I went into another 4 years of education. Desire to travel (that didn't work out lmao). items.question: How high was your workload during exchange?
How stressed were you during exchange?
Favourite part of going on exchange?

answers.label: Being able to travel to over a dozen different countries during the school term
Exploring the world with my significant other with basically no educational worries
Getting to experience life in Europe
Getting to explore Singapore - I would choose different places to visit or things to do on a whim, including squeezing things in right after school
Having the time to travel not just in Hong Kong but also neighbouring countries (Asia tour !)
Making friends and learning about what student life was supposed to feel like for the first time in my life
Meeting new people and exploring a different continent
Opportunity to travel and explore Europe
Reduced stress from school, the country I was in was also extremely nice and I met some great people but the importance of not dying from coursework can't be overstated

labels: Being able to travel to over a dozen different countries during the school term

Opportunity to travel and explore Europe
Reduced stress from school, the country I was in was also extremely nice and I met some great people but the importance of not dying from coursework can't be overstated

items.question: Hardest thing about going on exchange?

answers.label: Bad transit by university making it hard to get around
Balancing co-op search with having fun
Being away from friends and VISA issues
Dealing with Waterloo-related work (capstone course/SE 390, job search) with a 12-hour time zone difference. The interview schedule was... not good.
Getting visa stuff sorted was really annoying

I think it was really just all the research that I had to do in 2B co-op and leading up to it, the paperwork and logistics to be done the summer right before it. Other than that facing being ill in a foreign country.
Initially settling into a new country
Living alone and abroad, not knowing anyone
Living so far and in a different time zone than everyone im close to
Not knowing a single person going with me
Not knowing anyone else and throwing yourself into a foreign place (culture shock)
Nothing really - there were some logistical challenges, but they're not hard. There's plenty of guidance and support available.
being away from friends and family

labels: Bad transit by university making it hard to get around

Nothing really - there were some logistical challenges, but they're not hard. There's plenty of guidance and support available.
being away from friends and family

items.question: Most valuable thing you took away from exchange?

answers.label: Breaking the waterloo syndrome of all work no fun - plus becoming more spontaneous
How to be more independent, people find a way to make it through tough times
How to get out of the SE rat race and why money doesn't matter as much as you'd think.
I didn't enjoy the European lifestyle as much as I thought I would
Independence and making new friends all by myself in a new city!
Life is short do the new fun things when you’re young while you can
School matters less than the experiences you take away while at school
The ability to make travel plans really quickly and figure out how to use public transportation in Europe lmao
The world is so so big, there are many experiences and opportunities outside of North America and ppl from around the world live such different lives
Theres so much to see and experience in the world

You can do a lot (and have a lot of fun) if you're willing to open your mind and put in the effort to make things happen! Trying different things can surprise you, usually in a good way if you're willing to take a good lesson out of things.
a sense of freedom that stays with me
as much as I'd like to say cultural knowledge or new experiences, it was probably avoiding cs343
perspective and space to reflect on a different lifestyle (madrid life is very slow), spending time/money and what’s important

labels: Breaking the waterloo syndrome of all work no fun - plus becoming more spontaneous

items.question: Stream 8 vs 8x vs 8y
What language do you use for programming interviews?
Have you ever been late to a co-op interview?
Have you ever missed a co-op interview?
Any fun interview stories?

answers.label:
1. Had an interviewer grill me for 25 minutes on random trivia about programming practices. Dodged a bullet.
2. Was asked about the purpose of a company’s primary product, commented on the company’s architecture. The interviewer said “wow” under his breath and wrote something down. It was the only thing he wrote down, and the only time this ever happened (I’m not cracked) and I got the job :)

A QA position I interviewed for in 1B held all of the interviews on the same Zoom meeting. I joined a few minutes early as I usually do but ended up having to awkwardly sit through the last moments of the previous candidate's interview. The same thing then happened to me at the end of my interview. The interviewer asked me straight up whether I would take the job or not, and I'm pretty sure the fact that I hesitated was the reason why I wasn't ranked.
Absolutely bombed a simple interview because I forgot to study DB stuff, like who just remember what left outer join does for fun. Just show me the ven-diagram picture man
Had an interview scheduled for 12PM, interviewer wanted to reschedule to 4PM, I said okay. By 2PM they sent me an email saying they had found a candidate.

I had to do my final Engineering Manager interview (which was an additional EM round bc the first guy talked too much and we couldn’t get deep enough in my resume) and I was in exchange with my SIGNIFICANT OTHER and we got brunch right before with a friend but I was nervous so I drank 2 mimosas and made them go on a walk around the neighbourhood - best interview of my life actually
I instantly wrote 69 as a test case when asked for larger inputs. I don't think the interviewer noticed and I quickly changed it

I interviewed for a ML position, without having any experience in ML. 10 minutes in the interview and the interviewer asked me to explain a ML concept in simple terms. I debated for a second and then just decided to be honest and told him "I don't know". i told him that I could google it and tell him, but I wouldn't know it otherwise. he started laughing, we had a good laugh about it and then he just ended the interview 15 minutes early.
I once called a company a used car salesman. I also once arrived 30 minutes late, asked for the salary and then said "oh oops sorry i didnt want it anyways".

In-person interview at CECA. I was there 15 minutes early, my interviewer was also there on time. The person at the front desk at CECA somehow didn't call my name until I went and asked about my interview, by which point I was 30 minutes late to the interview.
Interviewed with an upper year SE for his company on waterlooworks at the time

Interviewer asked if I was adventurous, or liked trying new experiences/foods, and I mentioned I was planning to try Takoyaki, aka octopus balls. The interviewer gave me a funny look, and asked "as in... like the part of the octopus?" Found it very funny after I clarified they're balls of batter and octopus meat.
Interviewer left me alone in the interview call for 30 minutes

Interviewer showed up and asked a question I just got asked 30 minutes ago by the previous interviewer (2 interviews back to back for the same company). Was truthful and told them I got asked the question already so they gave me another one. Ended up getting by offer :)
Joined one of my interviews on my alt email as Bob Ross

Multiple occasions where I would take the interview first and launder the questions to friends :P

I once did 60+ interview rounds during a single hiring cycle

I once interviewed at [NYC quant firm] where my interviewer paused the interview halfway through to go and yell at someone over the phone for five minutes (their video was on but thankfully the audio was muted)

Met lots of psycho interviewers at quant firms D:

One startup once asked me an IOI question from the early 2000s :grimace:
My first job interview, I fumbled the reverse palindrome question despite having solved it before.
One company asked me animal care questions

One of the reasons I got my first co-op was because when my interviewer recommended a book, I pulled it off my bookshelf behind me with the bookmark still in it and said I enjoyed it as well. I never finished reading that book.
One time I brought up esports / gaming with my interviewer and we ended up talking about it for the rest of the interview
One time the fire alarm went off in the middle of my interview and I had to evacuate the building. And then I was doing the interview outside while being evacuated from the building.
The leetcode had an O(n) solution and I implemented it in O(n^2), I bombed both questions so badly that the interviewers were speechless and barely tried to say anything.
forgot about an interview to come 15 minutes late and not get the job
interviewer never joined the call, ended up postponing to another day. fire alarm repair ppl enter my room while I'm interviewing and do their thing in the background while I code.
my first ever interview was a technical interview where they had me code in google docs. i did not get the job
while on exchange, I had to do an interview in the middle of the airport and needed to pause halfway because someone was asking for directions in spanish (no hablo espanol lo siento!!).

items.question: What was your co-op role?
How many employees worked at your company?
Which industry was your co-op employer in?
What programming languages did you use at your co-op?
What would you rate your co-op experience?
Favourite overall co-op city?
Anything else co-op related you'd like to add?

answers.label: All my coops were remote, I hated it at first but learned to make the most of it by travelling towards my last coop.
Apply early, apply often, apply to things you don't think you'll get

Ask questions early on because you will look MORE dumb the later you ask speaking from EXPERIENCE

Go for breadth in co-ops and don’t be afraid to take on funky projects because they stand out in a good way !

Prioritize natural friendships in your internships and genuinely make sure you are having fun or else you are doing it wrong 😑
Be ballsy, just straight up reject all the offers you are not interested in. Try to find a place you  feel like home
Cali overtime regulations are very intern-friendly! recommend maxing them out whenever possible
EU/Japan coops were totally worth it (although definitely was not revenue positive)

For work: don't be afraid to ask questions, but try and figure the problem out yourself before going to someone for help, if only so that you can tell them what you've already tried, which will help point both of you in the right direction. Telegraph every design decision you make and document everything you do.

Outside of work: try, as much as you can, to approach everything with excitement and an open mind. Co-op is a wonderful opportunity to meet new people, try new things, and experience new cities - take advantage of that whenever you can!
I wish I applied external more, I wish I fixed my resume earlier, I wish I tried to learn from my coops instead of just doing my hours
Infra is the way. Never give your soul to a company. Make friends, go out, see the world.
Keep applying, even after exams finish
Never give up and stay ambitious

To the people looking for their first co-ops - this is hard! Harder than they make it seem! Don't get too discouraged if you don't make it in the first round, or second, or at all - as long as you don't get complacent, these situations are recoverable.
Try and find work that you like and are good at :D
Try to make local friends especially people who don't work in tech or go to Waterloo
Wish I'd applied more to in-person jobs and/or external stuff but at the end of the day it is what it is
a lot of what you learn is really what you make of the co-op, go off and do ambitious things, ask to be put on projects you find interesting, you get to control your growth
really enjoyed living in different cities and the diversity of experience — would definitely recommend prioritizing trying new things on co-op!

items.question: What device do you most frequently use to take notes?
What note-taking software do you use?
Which List 1 ATE did you take?
Opinions on List 1 ATEs
Which List 2 ATE did you take?
Opinions on List 2 ATEs
Which List 3 ATE did you take?
Opinions on List 3 ATEs
Favourite core course?
Least favourite core course?
Most useful core course?
Why did you think this course was useful?

answers.label: A lot of practice solving coding problems that proved to be useful in interviews later on
Core Algorithms / Leetcode course. Helped with a lot of the interviews
Directly helped me with multiple interviews/offers.
Gave me a good understanding of algorithms, which was generally useful
Helped develop a fundamental understanding of core data structure
Helped me understand how coding actually works
In addition to concurrency in general, it makes you think about your code which is a good habit to get into
Jeff zarnett unironically taught us so much useful garbage
Learned how to think about operating systems & the way our computers work
Learning concurrency from peter buhr is a pleasure.
My job requires knowledge of concurrency concepts
Realistically, most of the things I interact with deal with algorithms we commonly use
SE 350 acts as the last course in the chain of courses about how computers work, and fundamentally changed how I understand how computers work.
Taught the inner workings of compilers in a very organized and understandable fashion, very hands on in the exact right places, very gratifying progress wise
Very applicable to real world, also sufficiently theoretical to be helpful
concurrency was much more interesting to have an introduction to
intro to a lot of important cs topics, ie more in depth run time analysis
object oriented programming also forcing u to make a project
very applicable for understanding programs, wouldn’t have learned this otherwise on my own
very useful for interviews, also I used a DFS at work once
wholistic view of how computer work

answers.label: CS 449 should have been taken before SE 390 because it helps with FYDP planning

Doing well in school requires sacrifices, you may not enjoy the courses you're forced to take, so be wary of what you choose to spend your time on. If a course is tricky, it probably is (and has been for past cohorts too).

CS and MATH courses tend to be more well-organized than ECE courses.
Make sure you can work well with your FYDP group, or be very good at mediation.
FYDP might be my least favourite part about SE tbh
Have the courage to do what feels right (drop into CS earlier)
I was in a team of 3 for fydp and 1 person was remote the whole time so basically 1 person team.
In 3B my term average was my 1 course (SE 390) due to being on exchange
Least useful course was a tie between ECE 106 and CHE102
Make sure you're friends with everyone in your FYDP group!
Overload early and overload often! I didn't enjoy the 3a/3b/4a/4b se courses SE 465, SE 464, SE 463 + fydp at all, felt very strongly that they are very useless

The FYDP courses should improve. SE 490 and 491 have no learning outcome.
Have a mandatory resume workshop/critique in 1A for all students, supported by the university program.

don't study in SE lmao go to CS literally trust me you will regret SE
make sure your FYDP team is on the same page on how far you're taking the project/putting effort
on exchange during 3B, no course average available besides SE390
take online courses to ease your workload, take courses you like to enjoy school
we had some good profs and some bad profs

items.question: What non-technical extracurriculars did you actively participate in?
Are there any extracurriculars you regret not joining? Why?

answers.label: Art, music, games, etc. would have been so fun! I wish i made more time for fun clubs
Fencing. Sport I enjoy, but covid made me stop and never picked back up.
Hack the North organizing, seemed fun
I didn't do any clubs, wish we had the time to
Maybe some technical ones like for the design teams, but I did not have much time commit.
No, tbh I kinda regret joining some because of how thin they spread me out
Not a regret but I will always have a burning hatred for Watanomous for rejecting me 3x in a row :)
Poker Club. Would have been fun.
Probably would have been nice to join some design team, or maybe the game design club, feels like I would've benefitted a lot, both from having a bit more experience and meeting new people.
Wish I did Muay Thai earlier, also just more clubs in general, also wish I joined soccer intramurals because I watched them and they aren't actually that good
Wish I did more but covid made it suck!
blueprint seemed really cool, get to work on tech for good

answers.label: All the various SE-events we've held and the dance party and talent shows in particular.
First ECE105 lecture online with prof Mansour
Going to events with cohort friends.
Hanging out with friends late in EIT - it's where all the interesting conversation starts coming out
IRS was fun (can't remember that far back)

Putting up the SE25 banner on prank night, SE bonfires in 2B, studying in EIT, performing & watching others perform at Bim Bortons Coffeehouse (all iterations), hanging out with cohort friends during co-op terms
SE campfire days back in second year
Seeing the northern lights in a hot spring in Iceland on exchange, and then having soup after
Stealing a bunch of Aerons from an office that was closing down with friends
The shenanigans going on in the SE lounge in EIT.
Working on the grade prank - seeing people come in throughout the day to make their handprints on the banner, and seeing everyone gather for the setup night!
grad prank was very fun and chaotic :)
walking to cambridge multiple times on a whim

answers.label: ...You should treasure the encounters you have and the people you meet. Death isn’t the only goodbye in this life. -Frieren: Beyond Journey's End
Be kind but don't be a pushover
Believe you can and you're halfway there
Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends.
I wanted to give the folks a little sizzle
If it ain't broke, don't fix it.
If you just start it's amazing what you can achieve...
It's not about how hard you get hit, but how hard you can get hit and keep moving forward
It’s not about the end result, but about the friends and memories you made along the way

No one is going to push you except yourself
OR 
someone less competent is living your dream right now
Sometimes, I prefer to do nothing. Doing nothing doesn't have that confusion - Magdy Salama, ECE 192
The future belongs to those who believe in the beauty of their dreams.
Tough times don't last, tough people do.
You can't connect the dots looking forewords, you can only connect them looking backwards. So you have to trust within yourself that the dots will somehow connect in the future. - Steve Jobs
everything is temporary / good things take time
rage, rage, against the dying of the light
suffering or happiness are choices we get to make
the best time to plant a tree was a hundred years ago, the second best is now

items.question: Best life hack/tip?

answers.label: Always have a tide pen on you
As soon as you can, start using Google calendar religiously
Be friends with people who are smarter than you!
Figure out a calendar/task setup early

Find every way to save every dollar/every cent by using the right combination of credit/debit products/spending habits. Spend BIG on things you LOVE and small on things that you don't. File the inside of your USB-C ports when they stop working and they'll work again.

If you feel like everyone hates you, take a nap. If you feel like you hate everyone, eat something.

Make cupcake sandwiches by detaching the bottom of the cupcake and putting it on top of the frosting. This creates a consistent ratio of cake to frosting with every bite!
Learn how to cook for yourself
Learn the campus map and all of its tunnels/bridges, so you never get lost trying to go to class. (or just know someone who does)
Put your bread in the freezer
Recognize what makes you happy and chase it
School is so much easier when you work with people around you, especially for studying!!
You can boil eggs in a kettle
don't give a sht about people you don't care about

don't take no for an answer 
OR
often when you live and make decisions sub-conciously, you do it through the built-up lens of your doubts and fears
if you keep running out of [x] and it's not that expensive. buy more! (esp stuff like dishes, plates, etc.)
sleep and food do fix things

items.question: Advice you'd give your first-year self?

answers.label: Be proactive, and don't fall behind! Early habits matter.
Create side projects early on and explore more by doing more projects and prioritize leetcode. Stay happy and enjoy the process
Do leetcode earlier, put more effort into applying to coops, do coops with the intention to learn

Don't change yourself to conform to a group, no matter how much success "appears" to be guaranteed if you conform, keep your own unique brand and charm and grow it rather than actively diminish it. Prioritize and cut out noise if you have to, and protect your identity.

Don't overthink it. You can worry a bit when things go wrong, but don't worry too much, and for too long. With an open mind, and some creative thought, you'll see possibilities you wouldn't have imagined, although it'll take time to get to that point.
Your life will be so much better if you stop seeking external validation for your problems. Also, start trying to be less defensive now
Just keep going, and make good friends
Life is never that serious, take care of your health and keep loved ones close

Looking for co-ops based on their ratings on WW is the wrong move. Location and pay are the only things you should care about (unless the company sucked during the interviews). Try a bit harder at things that actually matter to you, and less hard at things that you don’t feel great about but are driven by fear to stay ahead on.
Meet more people. Don't be afraid of failure.
Seek advice on building a good resume. Start planning for graduate school early.
Swap to CS/ go on exchange/ talk to more people
Take advantage of every opportunity more
Things do get better with time
Trust the process and keep your head in your own shoes
You have so much going for you, don't ever be afraid to put yourself out there and see what happens
You'll be alright in the end
finding interpsonal relationships is a sampling problem, you should importance sample and your sample count
go out more, uni is a playground for you to live on the extremes before you find out who you really are
good things come to those who persevere. don't give up so easily, even if it feels hopeless
keep a journal so you don't forget how awesome you are
life's about to throw you for a loop, enjoy! remember to sleep eat and exercise.
schools not the most important thing in life. Don't take the people around you for granted, they might not always be there.
take a deep breath, relax, and then get to work
to be more proactive about getting what I want
you can get what you want, but you have to fight for it

items.question: Anything else you'd like to add?

answers.label: Fail often and as soon as you can, the cost of failure compounds the further you go!
what a journey this has been!! pleasure is all mine

bro just say yes to more stuff and live like you're a professional you'll never regret it
AND
pick another undergrad than SE :3
Don't take your memories and experiences here for granted, they shape who you are from now on. So do your best to make good memories for yourself and others too

items.question: Did you ever commit SEcest with another SE25?
Did you at any point want to commit SEcest with an SE25?
What terms did you commit SEcest with another SE25?
Which term did you switch out of SE25?
Why did you choose to switch out of SE25?

answers.label: - to have a fresh new start with a different group of people - to take less courses to spend more time on research and to save money (international tuition is so expensive)  - liked my 4-th internship a lot and i was ok with returning there for another internship & full time - didn't want to do the final-year design project -  all my closest friends in se have transferred
Because I didn't want to take the follow up course for Math213 and genuinely wanted less workload to work on other things
Less engineering courses, more flexibility and free time.

The international tuition was too high for me to justify another year in online classes due to COVID. I found the job I wanted in my co-op and went full-time with them, and I've been working there for the last 3.5 years or so.
To pursue creating my own startup and growing business endeavors
Wanted to spend less time doing classwork and more time doing research, and the lighter CS courseload helps a lot with that

Because I didn't want to take the follow up course for Math213 and genuinely wanted less workload to work on other things
Less engineering courses, more flexibility and free time.

The international tuition was too high for me to justify another year in online classes due to COVID. I found the job I wanted in my co-op and went full-time with them, and I've been working there for the last 3.5 years or so.

items.question: Which program did you switch to?
Is there anything else you'd like to add?

answers.label: If not for COVID, everything might have been different, and from what I saw in online classes I think I would have enjoyed the classes especially from the Math Faculty. I don't really regret the path I have taken but UoW would have been a path I wouldn't have regretted as well.
Well, I think it would be better if SE can have more options for some ECE courses, because some of them may not be directly aligned with my goals, that is more software focused
